body_txt: A character in a piece of media is mentioned to still be alive, somewhere off-screen, but their actor has died, possibly during its production ; if the character wasn't already Put on a Bus , then they're permanently doing that, forever . While some studios or creatives would opt to recast the actor , especially if the work is in a non-live action medium, it's typically avoided out of respect for the actor. This is where this trope comes into play: to pay respect for the actor who portrayed them, this character isn't ever going to be brought back, unlikely to be seen or represented on-screen ever again, outside maybe a few mentions by the cast of what they're up to or communication methods that leave them away from the cast, like a letter. The creatives have decided to retire the character, where they're simply on a peaceful vacation, retired, settled down, or their adventures continue into eternity in-universe, while also leaving them in grace out-of-universe. Sometimes this is done rather than implying the character ended up being Killed Offscreen , the latter being seen as disrespectful, in some regard. The character is likely not to be mentioned outside of their present absence, maybe only to be brought up once and then never again after that. At worst, they've been put into deliberately dangerous but ambiguous fates , decided to leave on bitter terms, or something else came up and the characters can't contact them anymore. Regardless of the circumstance, the character has been retired from use and will not make any more explicit on-screen appearances. If the role needs to be filled, then the writers can introduce a Suspiciously Similar Substitute , a Legacy Character to fill their shoes (possibly a family member or their friend), or even someone with the same name who was named after who they're replacing . Many television series prior to the late 1960s did this, including Make Room For Daddy (Nana Bryant, who played Margaret Williams' elderly mother); Ward Bond, the original wagon master on Wagon Train ; among others. The character would simply stop appearing and normally, no future mention would be made of them; if there was, it would be brief, indirect references, usually as though they were still alive. Often, viewers probably never noticed that the character was missing, given the transition was often seamless or that the viewer was more concerned about story development or other characters. It was merely presumed , then, that the character the now-deceased actor portrayed was still alive, since no mention — explicit or otherwise — was made that there was a death. Certain sitcom writers, like David Croft, were personally opposed to mid-series re-casting, so major characters were subject to this following their original actors' death. A Sub-Trope of Character Outlives Actor , where it covers any instance of a character still continuing to exist in a series via various methods to give the illusion of the character still being around in-universe. See The Character Died with Him for cases where the character is killed off due to the actor's death, sometimes to acknowledge the cast being changed or dedicate the episode to that major cast member . Sometimes a series will change its mind and say that the character did end up dying like their actor, despite what was said before. Compare and contrast Actor Leaves, Character Dies , for when the character is killed off due to the actor leaving, but the actor is still very much alive, and Killed by Request , where an actor asks their character to be killed so they don't have to continue playing them. In a medium like video games or animation, if a character continues to appear after their voice actor has died, but their cameos are silent, not fully voice-acted, or done using repurposed voice clips in a completely different context , that's Actor Existence Limbo , not this trope, though this can also occur if the actor becomes unavailable for any circumstance, not just death. See also Outlived Its Creator , for when a series continues to exist despite the death of its original creator, and not the actors portraying the characters.
